does: This paper introduces Manifold Trajectory Kinetics (MTK), a detection method that identifies jailbreak prompts by analyzing how the neighborhood structure of a prompt's embedding evolves across layers of an LLM during inference. Rather than relying on fixed metrics in a single representation space, MTK models the language model as a kinetic system and tracks whether a prompt maintains proximity to benign neighborhoods or exhibits trajectories characteristic of jailbreaks. The method is evaluated against both pseudo-malicious prompts (benign text containing safety keywords) and adaptive attacks that explicitly optimize to evade detection, demonstrating robustness where prior fixed-metric approaches fail.
connects: This work extends prior jailbreak detection research that predominantly operated in fixed metric spaces—whether raw input features, gradient-based signatures, or hidden layer representations. It responds to the fundamental limitation that benign and malicious prompts are not universally linearly separable, building on recent observations that adaptive adversaries can exploit static detectors. The approach draws on manifold learning principles and dynamical systems thinking, connecting to broader literature on understanding how information transforms through deep networks. It also engages with concurrent work on adversarial robustness and alignment verification, positioning detection as a problem of understanding geometric structure preservation during computation rather than classification in a static embedding.
enables: This work opens avenues for deployment-time safety verification that remains robust to both distribution shift and adversarial optimization. By framing detection as a manifold geometry problem rather than a classification task, it suggests future defenses based on topological invariants and trajectory-level properties that may be harder to circumvent than point-wise metrics. The kinetic systems perspective could enable detection of more subtle misalignments and unsafe reasoning patterns that don't manifest as simple feature shifts. This approach may generalize beyond jailbreaks to detecting other forms of prompt manipulation, hallucination patterns, or distribution shift, and could inform training methods that directly optimize for maintaining benign neighborhood structure during inference.
